report:
GOAL PERIOD REPORT: March 2 – May 2, 2026

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. GOAL OUTCOME

1 of 2 goals met.

Goal Met: 1 of 2 | Weight: lost 11.9 lbs (11.9 lbs vs 9.3 lbs target) MET | Body Fat: lost 3.1% (16.38% vs 15.0% target) MISSED

The weight goal was exceeded by 2.6 lbs. Body fat came down meaningfully — from 19.48% to 16.38%, a drop of 3.1 percentage points — but ended 1.38 points above the 15% target. The BF miss is partly a function of lean mass loss alongside fat loss (more on that below), which limited how far the percentage could drop even as total weight fell.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

2. THE NUMBERS

Start weight:           139.3 lbs
End weight:             127.4 lbs
Weight lost:            11.9 lbs (target: 9.3 lbs) — EXCEEDED

Start BF%:              19.48%
End BF%:                16.38%
BF% lost:               3.10 percentage points (target endpoint: 15.0%) — MISSED

Start lean mass:        112.2 lbs
End lean mass:          106.5 lbs
Lean mass change:       -5.7 lbs

Estimated fat lost:     ~6.2 lbs (derived from weight and BF% delta)
Lean mass loss share:   ~48% of total weight lost came from lean tissue

Average daily calories: 1,412 (target: 1,442) — 2.1% under target, strong adherence
Calorie logging rate:   89% of days logged
Average daily protein:  126.9g (target: 135g; floor: 108g)
Protein at/above target: 42% of logged days
Protein at/above floor:  78% of logged days

Total workout sessions: 43 over 62 days
Average sessions/week:  4.9
Average strain score:   18.7
Sport breakdown:        Running (43 sessions), Weightlifting (36), Basketball (2), HIIT (1), Walking (1), Pilates (1)

Note: Running and weightlifting counts likely reflect overlapping or dual-tagged sessions rather than 79 distinct workouts across 43 sessions. The 4.9 sessions per week figure is the operative volume number.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

3. PROJECTION

Weight goal was met — no projection needed for weight.

Body fat goal (15.0%) was missed by 1.38 percentage points. At the observed rate of BF% decline across the final three weeks of the goal (roughly -0.44 percentage points per week, based on the 7-day rolling average moving from 17.48% on April 13 to 16.40% on April 27), reaching 15.0% from the current 16.38% would require approximately 3 additional weeks at that pace.

However, that projection assumes the same calorie deficit, training volume, and lean mass preservation rate continue — none of which are guaranteed. Fat loss rate is rarely linear, and as body weight decreases, the deficit required to sustain the same rate of loss shrinks unless calories are adjusted downward or training output increases. Treat the 3-week estimate as a floor, not a guarantee.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

4. WHAT DROVE RESULTS

Weight loss followed a clear stepped pattern rather than a smooth decline, and the input data supports several causal observations.

Phase 1 — Early rapid drop (March 2–12): Weight fell from 139.3 to 134.5 lbs in the first ten days, a drop of 4.8 lbs. Food logging had not yet started (March 2–7 show no calorie data), but training was active with strain scores in the 17–21 range. This initial drop likely includes a meaningful water and glycogen component from the onset of a calorie deficit combined with high training output. It is real weight loss, but the pace was not sustained, which is consistent with early-phase water shedding.

Phase 2 — Volatile mid-period and reset (March 13–24): A 3,416-calorie day on March 14 drove weight back up to 137.8 lbs on March 15 — the single largest single-day rebound in the dataset. Weight then gradually re-traced back to the low 130s by March 24–25. This stretch was the least efficient of the goal period: calorie volatility was high, protein was inconsistent (ranging from 53g to 156g), and the 7-day rolling BF average was essentially flat at 19.2–19.4% through mid-March before beginning to decline.

Phase 3 — Sustained break lower (March 24–31): Around March 24–25, something shifted. Weight dropped sharply to 131.2 lbs on March 25 (the lowest point to that date), and the rolling BF average began a steeper descent, falling from 19.41% (week of March 16) to 18.36% (week of March 23) and continuing to 18.02% by the week of March 30. Looking back 3–7 days, the inputs during March 18–24 show consistently lower calorie days (1,182 / 1,221 / 1,350 / 1,463 / 539 / 1,150) — several days well below target — combined with high strain scores (19–20 range) on workout days. The combination of a tighter effective deficit and continued high training output appears to have triggered this phase of accelerated loss.

Phase 4 — Stable plateau (April 1–12): Weight held in a tight band of 130.7–132.5 lbs for roughly two weeks. Calories during this phase averaged close to target (around 1,100–1,660 per day), protein was more consistently above the floor, and strain remained high. The rolling BF average continued declining from 18.02% to 17.80%, suggesting body composition was still improving even as scale weight plateaued. This is the clearest evidence in the data that fat was being lost during a scale stall.

Phase 5 — Final acceleration (April 13–May 2): From April 13 onward, weight broke below 131 lbs and declined steadily to a low of 126.8 lbs on April 23, closing the goal period in the 127–128 lb range. Protein adherence was notably higher during this stretch — the weeks of April 6 and April 13 averaged 140.1g and 141.6g respectively, both above the floor and closer to target than any prior two-week window. The rolling BF average dropped from 17.80% to 16.40% across these final four weeks, the steepest sustained BF decline of the goal period. The coincidence of improved protein intake and accelerated BF reduction is consistent with better lean mass signaling during the deficit, though the data does not allow a definitive causal claim.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

5. WHAT HELD YOU BACK

The BF% goal was missed, and the data points to two primary culprits.

Lean mass loss was the central problem. Losing 5.7 lbs of lean mass out of 11.9 lbs total means roughly half of all weight lost came from non-fat tissue. Even though absolute fat was lost (approximately 6.2 lbs), the lean mass decline limited how far BF percentage could fall. Had lean mass been preserved at, say, 110 lbs instead of 106.5 lbs, end BF% would have been closer to 15.7% — still short of target, but materially closer. The lean mass loss is likely attributable to a combination of protein falling below target on most days (only 42% of days hit 135g) and a calorie deficit that may have been too aggressive on some days relative to training output.

Protein inconsistency was the second factor. While the 78% protein floor adherence sounds acceptable in isolation, falling below 108g on 22% of logged days — during a period of high training volume and a meaningful calorie deficit — creates recurring conditions where muscle protein synthesis is undersupported. The low-protein days were not randomly distributed; they tended to cluster in the early and volatile mid-period (March 8–25), which is precisely when the most lean tissue was likely being lost. Several days in that window recorded protein intakes of 53g, 65g, 68g, 74g, and 77g — well below both the floor and any meaningful muscle preservation threshold given the training load.

The calorie logging gap (11% of days unlogged) is a secondary issue. Those unlogged days are concentrated in the first week (March 2–7), a period when weight was also falling rapidly. Without data, it's impossible to know whether those days were at deficit, at maintenance, or above — but the absence of logging at the outset likely contributed to the early volatility.

The March 14 outlier (3,416 calories) wiped out roughly four to five days of deficit in a single day. It was an isolated event and did not repeat, but it cost approximately one week of net progress during the most volatile stretch of the goal.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

6. MEAL PATTERNS

Calorie logging was strong overall at 89%, but the unlogged days were front-loaded (the entire first week), which means the goal started without a baseline. This is worth noting because the early rapid weight drop occurred during an untracked period, making it harder to understand what actually drove it.

Calorie volatility was high in weeks 1–3 and compressed significantly from week 4 onward. The range narrowed from a week-1 swing of 777–3,416 calories to a week-7+ range of approximately 904–1,732. The tighter the band got, the more consistently weight moved in the right direction.

Severely under-fueled days (below 1,000 calories) appeared on seven occasions throughout the goal: March 12 (777 cal), March 15 (1,052 cal — borderline), March 16 (962 cal), March 24 (539 cal), April 1 (934 cal), April 3 (1,111 cal — borderline), April 6 (1,018 cal). These days disproportionately drove low protein intake as well, since the two metrics are correlated. On days under 1,000 calories, protein averaged well below the 108g floor. Sub-800-calorie days on a training schedule with average strain of 18.7 represent a notable underfueling risk, particularly for lean mass retention.

High-calorie days (above 1,800) clustered in the first half of the goal: March 8 (1,867), March 9 (1,634), March 13 (1,928), March 14 (3,416), March 21 (1,930), March 22 (2,137), March 27 (1,634), March 28 (1,889). From April onward, only two days exceeded 1,800 calories (April 4 at 2,295 and April 24 at 1,927), and neither caused a sustained weight disruption the way March 14 did.

The data suggests a pattern of compensatory eating: very low days followed by or preceding higher days. The week of March 9 is the clearest example, with a swing from 1,150 to 1,928 to 3,416 to 1,052 across four consecutive days. This kind of volatility appears to have modestly blunted early progress without materially improving it.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

7. WORKOUT PATTERNS

Training consistency was a clear strength. 43 sessions over 62 days, averaging 4.9 per week, with only one week (the final partial week of April 27–May 2) falling to 3 sessions. Every other week logged exactly 5 sessions. This is the most consistent input metric in the entire dataset.

Strain held steady and high. The average strain of 18.7 is notable — this is a significant daily training load. Strain scores rarely dipped below 17 on workout days, and several sessions registered 20–24, indicating consistent high-output work. There were no extended low-effort stretches. The one anomalous low-strain session (11.86 on April 11) appears isolated.

The sport mix was dominated by running and weightlifting, which together account for virtually all sessions. The 43 running and 36 weightlifting tags across 43 sessions suggests most sessions were combination efforts or that both activities were logged on the same day. Basketball appeared twice, HIIT and Pilates once each — these are negligible in the aggregate.

One potential concern: the consistent high-strain running volume alongside a calorie deficit creates significant metabolic demand. High-strain cardio accelerates calorie expenditure but also increases the risk of lean tissue breakdown when protein is simultaneously undersupported. The periods where protein fell below 100g and strain was 19+ (e.g., March 10, March 24, March 25) are the days most likely to have contributed to lean mass loss.

Rest day distribution appeared adequate — approximately 2–3 rest days per week throughout, which is appropriate for the strain levels being maintained.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

8. LESSONS FOR NEXT GOAL

Protein is the highest-leverage adjustment available. You hit the 135g target only 42% of days. The clearest difference between weeks where BF% declined faster (weeks of April 6–27, averaging 138–145g protein) and weeks where it stalled (weeks of March 9–16, averaging 94–119g) is protein intake. For the next goal, treat 135g as a hard floor, not a ceiling. On days where calories are low, prioritize protein above all other macros — this is where the data shows the most consistent correlation with better body composition outcomes.

Eliminate sub-800-calorie days entirely. The 539-calorie day on March 24 and the 777-calorie day on March 12 did not appear to produce lasting weight benefits (weight rebounded modestly within 1–2 days in both cases) and likely contributed disproportionately to lean mass loss given concurrent high-strain training. The calorie target of 1,442 is already in a deficit — there is no documented benefit in this dataset from going to extreme lows, and the protein data on those days confirms they were not high-quality deficit days.

Start logging on day one. The first seven days of this goal produced the most volatile weight and calorie data, and none of it was tracked. The absence of early data meant no feedback loop existed during a phase when habits are being established and early decisions are most impactful.

Control the high-calorie outlier days. The March 14 event (3,416 calories) was the single most costly input event in the dataset. One day at that level cost more in net deficit terms than any other single variable. This was an isolated event and didn't recur, which is a positive signal — but structuring pre-emptive guardrails around high-risk social or event eating situations would be worth planning for in the next goal period.

The plateau from April 1–12 (scale flat, BF still declining) is a useful reference point. If the scale stalls in a future goal, the data here shows that continued calorie adherence and protein near target produced real compositional change even without weight movement. This period is evidence that the scale alone is an incomplete progress signal.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
